ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a combined Japanese udon and pasta set meal. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest parent covering the dominant noodle component.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes - pasta and noodle dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers composite pasta and noodle dishes; used as closest parent for this mixed udon-pasta set menu. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for a Japanese fusion set meal of this type.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for a Japanese udon and pasta set meal combination.

main_ingredients: protein component (chicken, pork, or seafood depending on variant)

serving.natural_language_portion: One Japanese set meal typically consists of a bowl of udon noodles and a pasta dish, totalling approximately 450 g including broth, noodles, protein, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Udon noodles cooked (SR Legacy 20110) and Pasta cooked (SR Legacy 20121), combined weighted estimate for mixed set meal
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a Japanese udon-and-pasta set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy data for cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 20110) and cooked pasta (FDC ID 20121), with a protein component (chicken breast, cooked, FDC ID 05064) and vegetables, reflecting a typical restaurant set meal composition. Approximate ratio used: 40% udon noodles, 30% pasta, 20% protein, 10% vegetables and sauce. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this food category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Udon noodles (GI 55) and pasta (GI 41–49), composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a Japanese udon-and-pasta set meal. GI estimated as weighted composite: udon noodles GI ~55 (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; University of Sydney GI Database) and cooked pasta GI ~45 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted average at approximately 40% udon and 30% pasta contribution yields composite GI ~55. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 48.3, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 19.5 × 4.5 = 87.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from broth and soy-based sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this set meal as the udon broth and sauces may contribute approximately 1260 mg sodium per serving (450g), approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming less broth is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from udon and pasta noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should note that a full set meal serving provides approximately 88g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~48, which is substantial; portion control, choosing smaller servings, and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This set meal is relatively low in saturated fat (~1.8g per 100g) making it a reasonable choice for cardiovascular health, though the sodium content from broth warrants attention; opting for lighter broth and lean protein components supports a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Japanese set menu combining udon noodles and pasta dishes, as served at Japanese-fusion restaurants in Singapore such as To;asta on Circular Road. Udon are thick, chewy wheat-flour noodles originating from Japan, typically served in a dashi-based broth with toppings such as tempura, fish cake, or soft-boiled egg. The pasta component reflects a Japanese-Italian fusion style common in contemporary Japanese dining, where al dente pasta is prepared with Japanese-influenced sauces such as mentaiko (spicy cod roe), wafu (Japanese-style dressing), or miso cream. The set meal format provides a complete dining experience with a noodle course and a pasta course, often accompanied by a small side or salad. Nutritionally, the meal is carbohydrate-dominant with moderate protein from the protein toppings and relatively low fat. The sodium content is a key clinical consideration due to the broth and soy-based sauces. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low category), though the glycemic load per full serving is substantial at approximately 48 due to the large carbohydrate portion. This food is relevant for dietary counselling in the context of Type 2 Diabetes management, hypertension, and general carbohydrate-aware eating. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Japanese restaurant scene.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a Japanese udon-and-pasta set meal as served at this specific restaurant. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy data for component foods. GI is estimated from analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is low due to the composite and estimated nature of all nutritional values.
notes: This record represents a Japanese set menu combining udon noodles and pasta, as offered by To;asta - Circular Road on field survey data (food_id GD003374). The food_name has been expanded from the generic 'Set Menu' to 'Japanese Set Menu (Udon and Pasta)' to provide a canonical, non-ambiguous English identity. Nutrient values are estimated composites from USDA SR Legacy data and should be treated as indicative only; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB/USDA entry for this specific dish would improve confidence. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the component foods (cooked noodles, pasta, lean protein, vegetables) contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA SR Legacy source data. GI is set to 55 (low category, GI ≤ 55) based on weighted analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for udon noodles and pasta. gi_category is 'low' per the strict threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). Glycemic load per serving is calculated at 48.3 based on 450g serving size and 19.5g carbohydrate per 100g. The price of SGD 30.00 from the source record is noted but not included in the nutritional output as it is not a nutritional field. Allergen flags are indicative based on typical ingredients; actual allergen content depends on specific restaurant preparation.
